_comment: Drug interaction rule set. Keys are canonical lowercase drug names. Each entry maps to a list of interactions.

interactions.description: Additive CNS and respiratory depression. Co-administration significantly increases risk of apnea, hypoxia, and respiratory arrest.
interactions.management: Reduce doses of both agents when combined. Have airway equipment and naloxone/flumazenil immediately available. Monitor SpO2 continuously.

interactions.description: Additive CNS and respiratory depression. Combination can cause life-threatening respiratory depression.
interactions.management: Reduce both doses, monitor closely, have reversal agents ready.

interactions.description: Additive respiratory depression with increased risk of apnea.
interactions.management: Reduce doses, monitor respiration continuously.

interactions.description: Synergistic respiratory depression. Risk of apnea and cardiovascular depression.
interactions.management: Use lowest effective doses. Monitor SpO2, have naloxone and flumazenil available.

interactions.description: Synergistic CNS and respiratory depression. FDA Black Box Warning for opioid+benzodiazepine combination.
interactions.management: Avoid combination when possible. If required, reduce doses and monitor closely.

interactions.description: Potential for prolonged recovery and increased emergence reactions. Both lower seizure threshold at high doses.
interactions.management: Use lower ketamine doses when combined. Monitor for dysphoria or emergence reactions.

interactions.description: Amiodarone significantly increases digoxin plasma levels (up to 2x) by reducing renal and non-renal clearance.
interactions.management: Reduce digoxin dose by 50% when amiodarone is added. Monitor digoxin levels and for signs of toxicity (nausea, bradycardia, visual changes).

interactions.description: Additive negative chronotropy and dromotropy. Increased risk of severe bradycardia, heart block, and sinus arrest.
interactions.management: Use with extreme caution. Monitor ECG and vitals continuously.

interactions.description: Additive AV nodal suppression. Risk of complete heart block and severe bradycardia.
interactions.management: Avoid combination if possible. If required, monitor ECG continuously and have atropine/pacing available.

interactions.description: Amiodarone may potentiate anticoagulant effects of heparin, increasing bleeding risk.
interactions.management: Monitor aPTT or anti-Xa levels more frequently when initiating amiodarone.

interactions.description: Synergistic bradycardia and AV nodal block. Risk of severe hemodynamic compromise.
interactions.management: Avoid concurrent IV use if possible. Monitor ECG and blood pressure closely.

interactions.description: Beta-blockade prevents epinephrine from acting on beta receptors, leaving unopposed alpha stimulation. May cause severe hypertension and reflex bradycardia.
interactions.management: Use glucagon (5-10 mg IV) for beta-blocker reversal instead of epinephrine. Monitor ECG and BP.

interactions.description: Profound, potentially fatal hypotension. Both drugs cause vasodilation via cGMP pathway.
interactions.management: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION. Do not use within 24 hours of sildenafil/vardenafil or 48 hours of tadalafil.

interactions.description: Life-threatening hypotension when nitroglycerin given within 48 hours of tadalafil.
interactions.management: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION for 48 hours after tadalafil use.

interactions.description: Additive QTc prolongation. Increased risk of Torsades de Pointes and fatal arrhythmia.
interactions.management: Obtain baseline ECG. Monitor QTc. Avoid in patients with QTc >500 ms.

interactions.description: Additive QTc prolongation and Torsades de Pointes risk.
interactions.management: Avoid combination. If required, continuous cardiac monitoring mandatory.

interactions.description: Additive QTc prolongation. Both drugs independently prolong QT interval.
interactions.management: Monitor ECG, avoid in patients with QTc >450 ms.

interactions.description: Combination not indicated. Rocuronium should be used as alternative when succinylcholine is contraindicated, not in combination.
interactions.management: Use one NMB agent at a time. If succinylcholine given, do not add rocuronium until effect has worn off.

interactions.description: Additive CNS depression. Hypotension and respiratory depression are enhanced.
interactions.management: Reduce doses of both agents. Monitor airway and hemodynamics continuously.

interactions.description: Additive respiratory depression. Combination used in balanced anesthesia — requires dose reduction of both agents.
interactions.management: Reduce propofol induction dose by 20-30%. Monitor SpO2 and ETCO2.

interactions.description: Both agents are ototoxic. Concurrent use markedly increases risk of permanent hearing loss.
interactions.management: Avoid concurrent use if possible. If required, use lowest effective doses and monitor audiologically. Separate administration times.

interactions.description: Furosemide-induced hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia significantly increases risk of digoxin toxicity and fatal arrhythmias.
interactions.management: Monitor electrolytes closely. Replete K+ and Mg2+ aggressively. Monitor digoxin levels.

interactions.description: Furosemide-induced electrolyte changes (hypokalemia) may affect insulin dosing requirements and mask hypoglycemia.
interactions.management: Monitor glucose and K+ closely. Adjust insulin dose as needed.

interactions.description: NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation and may cause GI erosions, markedly increasing bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants.
interactions.management: Avoid concurrent use. If analgesic required, consider acetaminophen instead.

interactions.description: Combination dramatically increases bleeding risk. Typically heparin is held during tPA infusion and started after.
interactions.management: Do not start heparin during tPA infusion. Follow institution stroke/PE protocol for timing of heparin initiation.

interactions.description: Magnesium potentiates neuromuscular blockade, possibly prolonging paralysis unexpectedly.
interactions.management: Reduce succinylcholine dose. Monitor neuromuscular function. Have sugammadex or neostigmine available.

interactions.description: Magnesium potentiates non-depolarizing NMB, prolonging block and increasing risk of residual paralysis.
interactions.management: Reduce rocuronium dose. Monitor TOF (train-of-four) twitch response. Use sugammadex for reversal.

interactions.description: Intended interaction: naloxone reverses opioid effects. Risk of acute opioid withdrawal, severe pain, pulmonary edema, and hypertensive crisis with rapid reversal.
interactions.management: Titrate naloxone in small doses (0.04 mg IV increments) for non-arrest opioid reversal. Full dose (2 mg) appropriate for cardiac arrest.

interactions.description: Naloxone half-life (~60-90 min) shorter than fentanyl. Risk of re-narcotization after single naloxone dose.
interactions.management: Monitor for re-narcotization for at least 2-3 hours after reversal. Consider naloxone infusion for long-acting opioid overdose.

interactions.description: Additive vasopressor effects. Risk of excessive hypertension, tachycardia, and increased myocardial oxygen demand.
interactions.management: Monitor hemodynamics closely. Titrate doses carefully. Consider arterial line placement.

interactions.description: Additive catecholamine stimulation. Risk of severe tachycardia, hypertension, and arrhythmias.
interactions.management: Use lowest effective doses. Consider ECG monitoring. One vasopressor usually sufficient.

interactions.description: Intended combination for hyperkalemia treatment. Dextrose prevents hypoglycemia. Monitor glucose carefully.
interactions.management: Give together for hyperkalemia. Monitor blood glucose every 30-60 minutes for 4-6 hours. Repeat glucose if clinically indicated.

interactions.description: IV calcium can precipitate severe cardiac arrhythmias (VT/VF) in patients on digoxin by potentiating glycoside toxicity.
interactions.management: AVOID IV calcium in digoxin toxicity. If hyperkalemia with digoxin toxicity, use sodium bicarbonate and kayexalate instead. If calcium must be given, do so very slowly with continuous ECG monitoring.

interactions.description: Physical incompatibility: ceftriaxone-calcium precipitates can form, especially in neonates. Fatal pulmonary and renal precipitation reported in neonates.
interactions.management: NEVER mix in same IV line. Flush line thoroughly between administration. In neonates (<28 days), use different IV sites with at least 48 hours between administrations.

interactions.description: NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow, potentially increasing vancomycin nephrotoxicity.
interactions.management: Monitor renal function. Use lowest effective doses. Prefer acetaminophen if analgesic needed.

interactions.description: Additive sodium channel blockade and risk of AV block, bradycardia, and hypotension.
interactions.management: Avoid concurrent use in routine settings. If both needed (refractory VT), monitor ECG and hemodynamics closely.

interactions.description: Physical incompatibility: calcium carbonate precipitate forms when mixed. Do not infuse in same IV line.
interactions.management: Administer via separate IV sites or flush line completely between doses.

interactions.description: Additive anticholinergic effects: urinary retention, confusion, constipation, excessive sedation. Risk of anticholinergic syndrome.
interactions.management: Use lowest effective doses. Monitor for anticholinergic symptoms, especially in elderly patients.

interactions.description: Additive CNS depression and sedation.
interactions.management: Reduce doses and monitor sedation level.

interactions.description: Opposing pharmacological effects. TXA is antifibrinolytic; heparin is anticoagulant. Clinical significance depends on indication and timing.
interactions.management: Generally used for different indications. Monitor for signs of thrombosis when TXA is used in anticoagulated patients.
